title: When CoT Helps Most
concept: CoT is highly effective for math word problems, symbolic logic, and multi-step planning tasks, but adds unnecessary latency to simple classification. Boss explain ends with: 'Next up: Emergent Abilities.'

exercises.prompt: Why shouldn't we use Chain of Thought (CoT) for simple classification tasks?

options.text: It adds unnecessary waiting time and cost without improving the answer.
It makes the AI completely forget the question.
It only works for translation tasks.

exercises.explain: Chain of Thought helps the AI slow down and think through steps, which is perfect for complex logic but overkill for simple questions.

cards.title: Use the Right Tool
cards.body: Have you ever used a sledgehammer to tap a tiny nail? Using Chain of Thought for every single AI task is just like that. Let's learn when to use this powerful tool, and when to keep it simple!

cards.title: When to Use CoT
cards.body: Chain of Thought (CoT) prompting shines brightest on complex reasoning tasks like math word problems, logic puzzles, and planning. However, for simple tasks like sorting words or choosing labels, it just slows things down.

cards.title: The Long Division Scrap Paper
cards.body: Think of CoT like writing out the steps of long division on paper. For a hard math problem, you absolutely need to write it down. But if someone asks you 'Is 4 even?', you don't need a whole sheet of paper—you just say 'yes' instantly!

cards.title: Solving a Word Problem
cards.body: If you ask an AI: 'If John has 5 apples, gives 2 away, and buys double what he has left, how many does he have?', CoT helps it calculate: 5 - 2 = 3, then 3 * 2 = 6. Without CoT, it might guess wrong. But if you just ask: 'Is an apple a fruit?', CoT is a waste of time.

cards.title: More Steps Aren't Always Better
cards.body: Many believe that turning on Chain of Thought always makes AI answers better. In reality, for simple tasks like classifying emails as spam, CoT doesn't improve accuracy—it just makes you wait longer and costs more.

cards.title: CoT: When to Use It
cards.body: Let's compare when to turn Chain of Thought on versus keeping it off.

left.text: Math, logic, and planning puzzles that require step-by-step thinking.

right.text: Simple tasks like labeling data, sorting lists, or translating single words.

exercises.prompt: For which of these tasks is Chain of Thought (CoT) highly effective?

options.text: Detecting if a sentence is happy or sad
Checking if a website is online

exercises.explain: Multi-step math word problems require planning and intermediate calculations, making them perfect candidates for CoT.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Chain of Thought reasoning makes an AI respond faster.
exercises.explain: CoT adds extra thinking steps, which increases the time (latency) it takes for the AI to output the final answer.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word about why we avoid CoT for easy tasks.
exercises.sentence: Using CoT for simple classification adds unnecessary latency to the response.
exercises.explain: For simple tasks, skipping CoT prevents unnecessary waiting time (latency) and saves computer power.

exercises.prompt: Which of these AI prompts would benefit the most from Chain of Thought?

options.text: 'Is the word Elephant a noun or a verb?'
'If Jane is older than Bob, and Bob is older than Ted, is Ted older than Jane?'

exercises.explain: The first prompt is a simple label categorization (no logic needed). The second is a translation. The third prompt requires reasoning about sequence and logic, which benefits from CoT.

exercises.prompt: Imagine you are building a smart assistant. For which of the following tasks would using Chain of Thought (CoT) be most helpful and worth the extra waiting time?

options.text: Determining if a customer review is positive or negative
Solving a multi-step logic puzzle about seating arrangements
Detecting spam emails based on keywords

exercises.explain: Chain of Thought is a game-changer for complex, multi-step tasks, but using it for trivial questions just wastes time and money. Next up: Emergent Abilities.
